segments.speaker: designer
segments.label: Designer
segments.text:
# Sigma-1 Design Decision: Component Library & Design System

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-10
category: design-system
question: What approach should be used for the frontend component library and design system?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui + TailwindCSS 4 (PRD-aligned)"
description: Use shadcn/ui as the component library with TailwindCSS 4 for styling, exactly as specified in the PRD. shadcn/ui copies component source code into your project — you own every line. Components are built on Radix UI primitives under the hood, so you get the accessibility benefits of Radix without the abstraction cost. Pair with the existing Effect + TypeScript stack for validation and data fetching.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui default "New York" theme — clean, high-contrast, neutral palette. See https://ui.shadcn.com for the canonical reference. The equipment catalog would use Card, Table, and Sheet components; the quote builder would use multi-step Form + Dialog patterns; Morgan chat would use a custom Sheet/Drawer widget.
strengths:
  - Already specified in the PRD tech stack table — zero alignment friction with the rest of the team and existing documentation
  - Built on Radix UI primitives internally, so WCAG AA keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA attributes come free
  - Source-code ownership means full control over theming, animation, and dark mode without fighting library abstractions
  - Massive community momentum (200k+ GitHub stars), extensive examples for data tables (TanStack Table integration), forms (react-hook-form + zod/Effect Schema), and charts (Recharts)
  - TailwindCSS 4's new CSS-first configuration and `@theme` directive align perfectly with a token-based design system approach
  - CLI tooling (`npx shadcn@latest add`) makes incremental adoption fast — add components as needed, no upfront bundle cost
tradeoffs:
  - You own the component code, which means you own the maintenance — upstream updates require manual diffing or re-running the CLI
  - Default theming is intentionally minimal; requires deliberate design token work to express Sigma-1's brand (dark, production-forward, lighting industry aesthetic)
  - Some complex patterns (e.g., multi-select combobox, date range picker with availability overlay) require composition from primitives rather than importing a single component

OPTION_B:
label: "Radix UI Primitives + Custom Theme Layer + TailwindCSS 4"
description: Skip shadcn/ui and build directly on Radix UI primitives (@radix-ui/react-*) with a custom theme layer using TailwindCSS 4 and CSS custom properties. This gives maximum control over the component API surface and visual expression, at the cost of building more UI infrastructure yourself.
visual_reference: No pre-built visual reference — you'd be designing from scratch on top of unstyled primitives. Think Linear or Vercel's design language as aspiration, but requiring full implementation effort. The Framer candidate (framer-web-1, project ref fr_4j04q95j7s8n48kakevderq43c) could inform the visual direction, though the Framer SDK isn't currently available for direct export.
strengths:
  - Maximum design freedom — no default opinions to override, every pixel is intentional from day one
  - Radix primitives are the gold standard for accessible unstyled components (same ones shadcn/ui uses underneath)
  - Smaller dependency surface — you import only the primitives you need, no shadcn CLI tooling layer
  - If the brand demands a highly distinctive visual language (e.g., the dark, cinematic aesthetic visible on sigma-1.com), custom theming from primitives avoids fighting default styles
tradeoffs:
  - Significantly more upfront work: you need to build Button, Card, Table, Dialog, Sheet, Form, Select, Combobox, Toast, and ~20 other components before shipping anything
  - No community copy-paste ecosystem — every Stack Overflow answer and tutorial assumes shadcn/ui patterns; custom implementations are on their own
  - Directly contradicts the PRD specification, creating alignment overhead with the documented tech stack and any other developers onboarding to the project
  - The 533-product catalog, quote builder, and admin interfaces need data tables, forms, and complex interactions immediately — building these from scratch delays the critical path

OPTION_C:
label: "shadcn/ui + TailwindCSS 4, with Sigma-1 Custom Theme Package"
description: Same as Option A, but with an explicit upfront investment in a custom theme package: define Sigma-1 design tokens (colors, typography, spacing, radii, shadows) as a TailwindCSS 4 `@theme` configuration, override shadcn/ui's CSS variables to express the brand, and document the token system. This is Option A done right rather than a distinct approach.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui structure with a dark-mode-first palette inspired by sigma-1.com's current aesthetic — deep blacks (#0A0A0A), accent lighting colors (amber/warm white #F5E6CC for highlights, cool blue #4B8BF5 for interactive elements), high-contrast text. The Stitch mobile candidate (stitch-mobile-2) provides a modernized mobile layout direction to inform responsive token decisions.
strengths:
  - All benefits of Option A, plus brand coherence from day one rather than retrofitting later
  - Token system becomes the single source of truth — TailwindCSS 4's `@theme` directive makes tokens first-class CSS custom properties, consumable by shadcn/ui components and any custom components alike
  - Dark mode (essential for a lighting/visual production company) is designed into the token layer rather than bolted on
  - Creates a foundation that scales to the Expo mobile app when Phase 2 arrives (tokens can be mapped to React Native style values)
tradeoffs:
  - Adds 2-3 days of upfront token and theme work before the first feature ships
  - Requires design decisions about brand colors, type scale, and spacing before seeing them in context — some iteration will be needed
  - Same maintenance ownership tradeoff as Option A (you own the component source code)

REASONING:
I recommend Option A, and if the timeline allows even a day of upfront token work, Option C is the better version of the same choice. Here's why:

The PRD explicitly specifies "shadcn/ui + TailwindCSS 4" in the Website tech stack table. Deviating from this creates unnecessary friction — documentation mismatches, onboarding confusion, and the need to justify the change to every developer who reads the PRD. Option B (raw Radix primitives) gives you the same accessibility primitives that shadcn/ui already wraps, but requires you to build the entire component surface yourself. For a platform with 533+ products, a quote builder, financial dashboards, project management views, and an AI chat widget, that's weeks of UI infrastructure work before you ship a single feature.

The pragmatic reality: shadcn/ui *is* Radix UI — it's a thin styling and composition layer on top of the same primitives. Choosing between them is not an accessibility or quality decision; it's a velocity decision. Given the breadth of the Sigma-1 platform (equipment catalog, RMS, finance, social engine, Morgan chat), shipping speed for v1 dramatically outweighs the marginal design control gained from building custom primitives.

If I'm being direct: **Option A is the clear winner**, and I'd push for the Option C variant (custom theme tokens upfront) because a lighting/visual production company deserves a dark-mode-first, brand-expressive interface, and spending 2 days on token architecture saves 2 weeks of retrofitting later. The sigma-1.com crawl shows a dark, minimal, production-forward aesthetic — that's achievable with shadcn/ui's CSS variable system and TailwindCSS 4's `@theme` without touching Radix primitives directly.
```

---

**Verification checklist:**
- [x] Decision point dp-10 addressed
- [x] Each option has label, description, strengths, and tradeoffs
- [x] Recommendation stated (A, with C as the enhanced variant)
- [x] Reasoning references PRD tech stack specification, 533-product catalog scope, and project velocity needs
- [x] No strawmen — Option B is genuinely defensible for a team with more time and a stronger brand differentiation mandate
- [x] Visual references included (shadcn/ui site, sigma-1.com crawl data, Stitch mobile candidate, Framer project ref)
